petitioner_background: The petitioner is a physicist working as a postdoctoral research associate at Southern Methodist University (SMU), residing at Cornell University and participating in the CLEO particle detector collaboration (consisting of 150 researchers from 25 U.S. institutions). He obtained his Ph.D. from the Chinese Institute of High Energy Physics (IHEP), where he worked on the BES collaboration and was the first to observe more than 10 psi(2S) decay channels. He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the INA.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted citations of his work, referenced '230 results' of his work in the 2004 version of Review of Particle Physics and '33 results' that would miss the 2004 deadline, comments of reviewers of his submitted manuscripts, and several pages from an article in Physics Letters B with highlighted citations of CLEO collaboration results and other technical equations.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO withdrew any implication that typical citations can serve to meet this criterion. Articles which cite the petitioner's work are primarily about the author's own work, not the petitioner, so they cannot be considered published material about the petitioner. The Physics Letters B article does not mention the petitioner by name and cannot be considered an article 'about' the petitioner. Peer reviews of the petitioner's manuscripts are not published materials.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted e-mail messages between himself and other CLEO collaborators regarding 'paper committees.' On appeal, he submitted a letter from David G. Cassel, a professor at Cornell University, affirming the petitioner's participation on two CLEO Paper Committees and explaining that any publication by the collaboration must be validated in a rigorous process by other recognized experts within the collaboration before public presentation.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director determined this evidence was not indicative of the petitioner's preeminence in the field. One of the petitioner's references, James Alexander, indicated that CLEO produces 25 to 30 publications per year, each reviewed by a Paper Committee made up of CLEO collaborators. Serving on two such committees during a four-year period as a collaborator is not notable. Without evidence setting the petitioner apart from others, such as reviewing an unusually large number of articles, receiving independent requests from a substantial number of journals, or serving in an editorial position for a distinguished journal, the petitioner does not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner relied on his publication record and letters from collaborators. Letters from Zhengguo Zhao (then head of IHEP Physics division) described his first observation of more than 10 psi(2S) decay channels as important contributions to understanding strong interaction, and noted he was appointed to chair the BES psi(2S) Physics Analysis Group, the only Ph.D. student so appointed in BES's 20-year history. Alan J. Weinstein (Caltech) praised his measurements of decays of charmonium particles (psi(2S) and X_c0 mesons), first observation of a class of B meson decays (to D'*K* final state) relevant to CP violation in quark decays, and pioneering use of CLEO's new Ring Imaging Cerenkov Counter. Richard Stroynowski (SMU) ranked him as one of the best postdoctoral fellows at SMU and within the top 15-20% of postdoctoral researchers on CLEO. Ian P. J. Shipsey (Purdue, CLEO co-spokesperson) placed him at the top of fifteen research associates he had worked with. Yongsheng Gao (SMU) asserted he proposed the best procedure for particle identification, widely adopted by the collaboration. James Alexander (Cornell) asserted he contributed to technological underpinnings of elementary particle physics through detailed examination of charged particle tracking phenomena. Counsel argued his work was a continuation of work pioneered by Nobel Laureates Samuel Ting and Burton Richter, and noted the prestige of the journals publishing his work.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that opinions of experts cannot form the cornerstone of a successful claim, citing Matter of Caron International. Letters with mere assertions of widespread acclaim are less persuasive than those specifically identifying contributions with examples of influence. Any Ph.D. thesis must offer new and useful information, but not every researcher performing original research has inherently made a contribution of major significance. The AAO would not narrow the field to Ph.D. students or postdoctoral researchers, so comparing well with other Ph.D. students did not place him at the top of the field. Professor Weinstein's statements that his accomplishments were 'exceptional for new PhDs' and speculation that he would 'play an ever more significant role' suggested he had yet to reach the pinnacle of his field. Richard Galik noted that 'every collaborator spends time helping understand, maintain, and calibrate the experimental apparatus,' undercutting the significance of technical contributions. Working in a subfield pioneered by Nobel Laureates does not establish one's own acclaim — not every physicist focusing on relativity enjoys Einstein's acclaim. The significance of individual articles cannot be inferred from the publication. The record establishes CLEO's international prestige and the petitioner's contributions comparable to top postdoctoral researchers within the collaboration, but does not establish individual acclaim independent of CLEO's reputation, nor that other large accelerator collaborations recognize his work individually as a contribution of major significance.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of authorship of numerous published articles and published presentations, along with evidence of citations (though the format made it difficult to identify the citations as citations of articles authored by him). On appeal, counsel asserted the petitioner was the 'key' or corresponding author of 17 out of 80 of his published articles, noted the prestige of the CLEO collaboration and publishing journals, and noted citation of his work.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director had not separately discussed this criterion, referencing his discussion of the contributions criterion where he concluded the petitioner had not established his role in CLEO's published articles. The AAO found unsupported assertions of counsel do not constitute evidence (citing Matter of Obaigbena, Matter of Laureano, Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez). Based on the record, the AAO could only confirm five articles where the petitioner is the primary or corresponding author. Nevertheless, given the total number of articles authored and the heavy citation of some articles (though not those listing him as primary author), the AAO was persuaded he meets this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner relied on his role with BES. Professor Zhao asserted the petitioner was appointed to chair the BES psi(2S) Physics Analysis Group from January 2000 until his graduation, and was the only Ph.D. candidate appointed as chair of a physics group in BES's 20-year history. On appeal, Professor Zhao asserted that as chair, the petitioner was 'responsible for evaluation and determination of the quality of scientific discoveries before submission for publication,' and that the psi(2S) Physics Analysis Group 'is the most active group at BES and has published a series of significant scientific discoveries which help understand the strong interactions of matter.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The role suggests the petitioner ranks highly with other Ph.D. students, but the record is less clear that this role is indicative of or consistent with national or international acclaim. Professor Zhao did not indicate how many groups exist within BES or provide the hierarchy of BES, so the AAO could not determine whether every group chair serves a leading or critical role for BES as a whole beyond the obvious requirement that results for each group be competently evaluated prior to publication. While the AAO did not question BES's national distinguished reputation, the petitioner did not establish he meets this criterion through playing a leading or critical role for that collaboration.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO concurred with the director that the petitioner established only one criterion (scholarly articles) of the three required. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner had distinguished himself as a physicist to such an extent that he achieved sustained national or international acclaim or was within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The evidence indicates the petitioner shows talent as a postdoctoral research associate but is not persuasive that his achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field. The record establishes CLEO's international prestige and his contributions comparable to top postdoctoral researchers within the collaboration, but does not establish individualized acclaim independent of CLEO's reputation. The petitioner has not sustained the burden of proof, and the appeal is dismissed.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791 (Comm. 1988)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)
Walters v. Metro. Educ. Enters., 519 U.S. 202 (1997)
